concepts.title: Definition of periodic function
concepts.conceptBody:
Periodic Function

A function $\text{f}:\text{D}\to \text{R}$ is said to be a periodic function if there exists a positive real number $p$ such that $f\left( x+p \right)=f\left( x \right)$ for all $x\in D$. The least of all such positive numbers $\text{p}$ is called the principal period of $\text{f}$. In general, the principal period is called the period of the function e.g. sin $\text{x}$ and $\text{cosx}$ are periodic functions, each having period $2\pi $. $\left\{ \text{x} \right\}=\text{x}-\left[ \text{x} \right]$ is a periodic function, the period being 1 . The graph of $\text{x}-\left[ \text{x} \right]$ is as shown in the figure.

concepts.title: Rules needed to determine the period of periodic functions
concepts.conceptBody:
Rules for finding the period of the periodic functions

(i) If $f\left( x \right)$ is periodic with period $p$, then $af\left( x \right)\pm b$, where $a,b\in R\left( a\ne 0 \right)$ is also a

periodic function with period $\text{p}$.

(ii) If $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is periodic with period $\text{p}$, then $\text{f}(\text{ax}\pm \text{b}$ ), where $\text{a}\in \text{R}-\left\{ 0 \right\}$ and $\text{b}\in \text{R}$,

is also periodic with period $\frac{\text{p}}{\left| \text{a} \right|}$.

(iii) Let us suppose that $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is periodic with period $\text{p}$ and $\text{g}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is periodic with

period $\text{q}$. Let $\text{r}$ be the LCM of $p$ and $q$, if it exists.

(a) If $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ and $\text{g}\left( \text{x} \right)$ cannot be interchanged by adding a least positive number $\text{k}$,

then $r$ is the period of $f\left( x \right)+g\left( x \right)$.

(b) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ and $\text{g}\left( \text{x} \right)$ can be interchanged by adding a least positive number $\text{k}$ and if

$\text{k}<\text{r}$, then $k$ is the period of $f\left( x \right)+g\left( x \right)$. Otherwise $r$ is the period.

(iv) If $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right),\text{g}\left( \text{x} \right)$ are periodic functions with periods ${{\text{T}}_{1}},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}_{2}}$ respectively then; we

have, $\text{h}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{a}f\left( \text{x} \right)\pm \text{bg}\left( \text{x} \right)$ has period as,

\(\begin{cases}\mathrm{LCMof}\left\{\mathrm{T}_1, \mathrm{~T}_2\right\} & ; \text { if h }(\mathrm{x}) \text { is not an even function } \\ \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{LCM of }\left\{\mathrm{T}_1, \mathrm{~T}_2\right\} & ; \text { if h }(\mathrm{x}) \text { is an even function }\end{cases}\)

Note : (1) LCM of $\left( \frac{a}{b},\frac{c}{d},\frac{e}{f} \right)=\frac{\text{LCM }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( a,c,e \right)}{\text{HCF }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( b,d,f \right)}$

(2) LCM of rational with rational is possible

LCM of irrational with irrational is possible

But LCM of rational and irrational is not possible.

Following results may be directly used 

(i) $\text{sin}x,\text{cos}x,\text{sec}x$ and $\text{cosec}x$ are periodic functions with period $2\pi $.

(ii) $\text{tan}x$ and $\text{cot}x$ are periodic functions with period $\pi $.

(iii) $\left| \text{sin}x \right|,\left| \text{cos}x \right|,\left| \text{tan}x \right|,\left| \text{cot}x \right|,\left| \text{sec}x \right|,\left| \text{cosec}x \right|$ are periodic functions with period $\pi $.

(iv) $\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{\text{n}}}\text{x},\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{\text{n}}}\text{x},\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{\text{n}}}\text{x}$ and $\text{cose}{{\text{c}}^{\text{n}}}\text{x}$ are periodic functions with period $2\pi $ and $\pi $

according as $\text{n}$ is odd or even respectively.

(v) $\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{\text{n}}}\text{x}$ and $\text{co}{{\text{t}}^{\text{n}}}\text{x}$ are periodic functions with period $\pi $, whether $\text{n}$ is odd or even.

concepts.title: Finding the period of a function
concepts.conceptBody:
Finding the period of a function

Q) Find the period of the function $f\left( x \right)=\frac{1}{2}\left\{ \frac{\left| \text{sin}x \right|}{\text{cos}x}+\frac{\left| \text{cos}x \right|}{\text{sin}x} \right\}$ 

Solution : Period of $\left| \text{sin}x \right|,\left| \text{cos}x \right|=\pi $

Period of $\text{sin}x,\text{cos}x=2\pi $

Period of $\frac{\left| \text{sin}x \right|}{\text{cos}x}=$ L.C.M. of $\pi $ and $2\pi =2\pi $

Period of $\frac{\left| \text{cos}x \right|}{\text{sin}x}=$ L.C.M. of $\pi $ and $2\pi =2\pi $

Period of $\frac{1}{2}\left\{ \frac{\left| \text{sin}x \right|}{\text{cos}x}+\frac{\left| \text{cos}x \right|}{\text{sin}x} \right\}=$ L.C.M. $2\pi $ and $2\pi =2\pi $

Q) Find the period of the following functions : 
(i) $f\left( x \right)=\text{sin}x+\left\{ x \right\}$ 

(ii) $f\left( x \right)=\text{tan}\frac{x}{3}+\text{sin}2x$ 

(iii) $f\left( x \right)=\left| \text{sin}x \right|+\left| \text{cos}x \right|$ 

Solution : 

(i) $\text{ Here f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{sinx}+\left\{ \text{x} \right\}$

Period of $\text{sinx}$ is $2\pi $ and that of $\left\{ \text{x} \right\}$ is 1 . But the L.C.M. of $2\pi $ and 1 does not exist.

Hence $\text{sin}x+\left\{ x \right\}$ is not periodic.

(ii) Here $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{tanx}/3+\text{sin}2\text{x}$. Here $\text{tan}\left( \text{x}/3 \right)$ is periodic with period $3\pi $ and and

$\text{sin}2\text{x}$ is periodic with period $\pi $. Hence $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ will be periodic with period $3\pi $.

(iii) $\text{Here}f\left( x \right)=\left| \text{sin}x \right|+\left| \text{cos}x \right|$.

Now, $\left| \text{sin}x \right|=\sqrt{\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{2}}x}=\sqrt{\frac{1-\text{cos}2x}{2}}$, which is periodic with period $\pi $.

Similarly, $\left| \text{cos}x \right|$ is periodic with period $\pi $.

Hence, according to rule of LCM, period of $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ must be $\pi $.

But $\left| \text{sin}\left( \frac{\pi }{2}+x \right) \right|=\left| \text{cos}x \right|$ and $\left| \text{cos}\left( \frac{\pi }{2}+x \right) \right|=\left| \text{sin}x \right|\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ [see rule (3) part (b)]

Since $\pi /2<\pi $, period of $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is $\pi /2$.

Q) Which of the following functions are periodic ? Also find the period if function is periodic. 

(i) $f\left( x \right)=10\text{sin}3x$ (ii) $f\left( x \right)=a\text{sin}\lambda x+b\text{cos}\lambda x$ 

(iii) $f\left( x \right)=\text{sin}3x$ 

(iv) $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{cos}{{\text{x}}^{2}}$ 

(v) $f\left( x \right)=\text{sin}\sqrt{x}$ 

(vi) $f\left( x \right)=\sqrt{\text{tan}x}$ 

(vii) $f\left( x \right)=x-\left[ x \right]$ 

(viii) $f\left( x \right)=x\text{cos}x$ 

where $x$ is integral part of $x$ 

Solution : 

(i) $f\left( x \right)=10\text{sin}3x$

Let $f\left( T+x \right)=f\left( x \right)$

$\Rightarrow 10\text{sin}\{3\text{ T}+3\text{x})\}=10\text{sin}3\text{x}\Rightarrow \text{sin}\left( 3\text{ T}+3\text{x} \right)=\text{sin}3\text{x}$

$\Rightarrow 3\text{ T}+3\text{x}=\text{n}\pi +{{(-1)}^{\text{n}}}3\text{x}$, where $\text{n}=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots \ldots $.

The positive values of $\text{T}$ independent of $\text{x}$ are given by

$3\text{ T}=\text{n}\pi $, where $\text{n}=2,4,6,\ldots \ldots $.

$\therefore $ least positive value of $\text{T}=\frac{2\pi }{3}$

Hence $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is a periodic function with period $\frac{2\pi }{3}$

(ii) $f\left( x \right)=a\text{sin}\lambda x+b\text{cos}\lambda x$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & =\sqrt{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}}\left( \frac{a}{\sqrt{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}}}\text{sin}\lambda x+\frac{b}{\sqrt{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}}}\text{cos}\lambda x \right) \\ {} & =\sqrt{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}}\left( \text{cos}\alpha \text{sin}\lambda x+\text{sin}\alpha \text{cos}\lambda x \right),\text{ where }\text{ tan}\alpha =\frac{b}{a} \\ \end{array}$

$=\sqrt{{{\text{a}}^{2}}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}}\text{sin}\left( \lambda \text{x}+\alpha \right)$

Which is a periodic function of period $\frac{2\pi }{\left| \lambda \right|}$

(iii) $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{3}}\text{x}=\frac{3\text{sinx}-\text{sin}3\text{x}}{4}=\frac{3}{4}\text{sin}-\frac{1}{4}\text{sin}3\text{x}$

Sin $\text{x}$ is a periodic function of period $2\pi $ and $\text{sin}3\text{x}$ is a periodic function of period $\frac{2\pi }{3}$.

Now L.C.M. of $\frac{2\pi }{1}$ and $\frac{2\pi }{3}=\frac{\text{ L}\text{.C}\text{.M}\text{. }\text{ of }2\pi \text{ and }2\pi }{\text{ H}\text{.C}\text{.F}\text{. }\text{ of }1\text{ and }3}$

$=\frac{2\pi }{1}=2\pi $

Hence $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is a periodic function of $2\pi $.

(iv) $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{cos}{{\text{x}}^{2}}$

Let $\text{f}\left( \text{T}+\text{x} \right)=\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\Rightarrow \text{cos}{{(\text{T}+\text{x})}^{2}}=\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}$

$\Rightarrow {{(\text{T}+\text{x})}^{2}}=2\text{n}\pi \pm {{\text{x}}^{2}}$

From this no positive value of $T$ independent of $x$ is possible because ${{x}^{2}}$ on

R.H.S. can be cancelled out only when $\text{T}=0$

$\therefore \text{ f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is a non periodic function.

(v) $f\left( x \right)=\text{sin}\sqrt{x}$

Let $\text{f}\left( \text{T}+\text{x} \right)=\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \Rightarrow \text{ sin}\sqrt{\text{T}+\text{x}}=\text{sin}\sqrt{\text{x}} \\ {} & \Rightarrow \sqrt{\text{T}+\text{x}}=\text{n}\pi +{{(-1)}^{\text{n}}}\sqrt{\text{x}} \\ \end{array}$

This will give no positive value of $T$ independent of $x$ because $\sqrt{\text{x}}$ on R.H.S. can

be cancelled out only when $\text{T}=0$.

$\therefore \text{ f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is a non periodic function.

(vi) $f\left( x \right)=\sqrt{\text{tan}x}$

Let $\left( \text{T}+\text{x} \right)=\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\Rightarrow \sqrt{\text{tan}\left( \text{T}+\text{x} \right)}=\sqrt{\text{tanx}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ tan}\left( \text{T}+\text{x} \right)=\text{tanx}$

$\Rightarrow \text{T}+\text{x}=\text{n}\pi +\text{x},=0,\pm 1,\pm 2,\ldots .$

From this positive values of $\text{T}$ independent of $\text{x}$ are given by

$\text{T}=\text{n}\pi ,\text{n}=1,2,3$

$\therefore $ least positive value of $\text{T}$ independent of $\text{x}$ is $\pi $.

$\therefore \text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is a periodic function of period $\pi $.

(vii) $f\left( x \right)=x-\left[ x \right]$, where $\left[ x \right]$ is the integral part of $x$.

Let $f\left( T+x \right)=f\left( x \right)$

$\Rightarrow \left( \text{T}+\text{x} \right)-\left[ \text{T}+\text{x} \right]=\text{x}-\left[ \text{x} \right]$

$\Rightarrow \text{ T}=\left[ \text{T}+\text{x} \right]-\left[ \text{x} \right]=$ an integer

Hence, least positive value of $\text{T}$ independent of $\text{x}$ is 1 .

Hence $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is a periodic function of period 1 .

(viii) $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{xcosx}$

Let $f\left( T+x \right)=f\left( x \right)\Rightarrow \left( T+x \right)\text{cos}\left( T+x \right)=x\text{cos}x$

$\Rightarrow \text{ Tcos}\left( \text{T}+\text{x} \right)=\text{x}\left[ \text{cosx}-\text{cos}\left( \text{T}+\text{x} \right) \right]$

From this no value of $\text{T}$ independent of $\text{x}$ is possible because on R.H.S. one factor

is $\text{x}$ which is an algebraic function and on L.H.S. there is no algebraic function in

$\text{x}$ and hence $\text{x}$ cannot be cancelled out.

Hence $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is a non periodic function.

Q) Let $f\left( x,y \right)$ be a periodic function satisfying $f\left( x,y \right)=f\left( 2x+2y,2y-2x \right)$ for all 

$x,y$. Let $g\left( x \right)$ $=\text{f}\left( {{2}^{\text{x}}},0 \right)$. Show that $\text{g}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is a periodic function with period 12 . 

Solution : Given, $f\left( x,y \right)=f\left( 2x+2y,2y-2x \right)$

$\therefore f\left( x,y \right)=f\left( 2x+2y,2y-2x \right)$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & =f\left[ 2\left( 2x+2y \right)+2\left( 2y-2x \right),2\left( 2y-2x \right)-2\left( 2x+2y \right) \right]\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ putting }x=2x+2y \\ \And y=2y-2x \\ \end{array} \right] \\ {} & =f\left( 8y,-8x \right) \\ \end{array}\,$

Thus $\text{f}\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)=\text{f}\left( 8\text{y},-8\text{x} \right)$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & =\text{f}\left( -64\text{x},-64\text{y} \right) \\ {} & =\text{f}\left[ \left( -64 \right)\left( -64\text{x} \right),\left( -64 \right)\left( -64\text{y} \right) \right] \\ {} & =\text{f}\left( {{2}^{12}}\text{x},{{2}^{12}}\text{y} \right) \\ \therefore \text{ f}\left( \text{x},0 \right) & =\text{f}\left( {{2}^{12}}\text{x},0 \right) \\ \text{ Now, }\text{ g }\text{ (x) } & =\text{f}\left( {{2}^{\text{x}}},0 \right) \\ {} & =\text{f}\left( {{2}^{12}}\cdot {{2}^{\text{x}}},0 \right) \\ {} & =\text{f}\left( {{2}^{\text{x}+12}},0 \right) \\ {} & =\text{g}\left( \text{x}+12 \right) \\ \end{array}$

Hence $\text{g}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is a periodic function with period 12 .

Q) Let $f$ be a real valued function defined for all real numbers $x$ such that for some fixed a 

> $0,f\left( x+a \right)=\frac{1}{2}+\sqrt{f\left( x \right)-{{(f\left( x \right))}^{2}}}$ for all real $x$. Show that $f\left( x \right)$ is a periodic 

function. Also find its period. 

Solution : Given, $\text{f}\left( \text{x}+\text{a} \right)=\frac{1}{2}+\sqrt{\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)-{{(\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right))}^{2}}}\forall \text{x}\in \text{R}$

$\therefore\left\{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{a})-\frac{1}{2}\right\}^2=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})-(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}))^2$

$=-\left(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})-\frac{1}{2}\right)^2+\frac{1}{4}$

$\Rightarrow\left\{f(x+a)-\frac{1}{2}\right\}^2+\left\{f(x)-\frac{1}{2}\right\}^2=\frac{1}{4}$

$\therefore\left\{f(x+2 a)-\frac{1}{2}\right\}^2+\left\{f(x+a)-\frac{1}{2}\right\}^2=\frac{1}{4}$

$(3)-(2) \Rightarrow\left\{f(x+2 a)-\frac{1}{2}\right\}^2-\left\{f(x)-\frac{1}{2}\right\}^2=0$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{a})-\frac{1}{2}=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})-\frac{1}{2}$

${\left[\therefore \text { from (1), } \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{a})-\frac{1}{2}>0 \forall \mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{R}\right.}$

$\therefore \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{a})-\frac{1}{2}>0 \text { or, } \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})-\frac{1}{2}>0 \forall \mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{R}$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{a})=\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}) \forall \mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{R} \text { and fixed } \mathrm{a}>0$

Hence $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is a periodic function with period $2\text{a}$.

examples.title: The period of $f(x)=x-[x]$, if it is periodic, is
If $f(x)$ is periodic function with period $T$ then the function $f(a x+b)$ where $a\gt0$, is periodic with period
Function $f(x)=x-[x]$, where [ ] shows a greatest integer. This function is
